claim: TrueNAS shifts to annual major releases starting with TrueNAS 26 (beta in April 2026), built on Linux Kernel 6.18 LTS with OpenZFS 2.4 and full LXC container support as a CORE/Jails migration path.
quote: TrueNAS 26 will shift to annual releases... OpenZFS 2.4... LXC containers are fully supported, offering legacy CORE users with custom Jails a clear, stable migration path... Linux Kernel 6.18 LTS

claim: TrueNAS Community Edition (formerly SCALE) is the singular free open-source NAS platform after CORE/SCALE consolidation; CORE remains in maintenance mode only.
quote: TrueNAS Community Edition represents the culmination of a long process of effort consolidation... the singular, free, open source storage platform for non-enterprise users

claim: TrueNAS Connect (announced early 2026) bridges Community and Enterprise: free Foundation tier for one system, Plus tier $60/year for up to 3 systems with SSO, monitoring, and ransomware protection.
quote: Foundation: Available to all TrueNAS users as a no-cost perpetual license... Plus: Manage up to 3 systems for $60/year with centralized visibility, SSO, custom enclosures, and alerts

claim: Unraid moved to subscription-style pricing on March 27, 2024: Starter $49 (6 devices), Unleashed $109 (unlimited), Lifetime $249; Starter/Unleashed include 1 year of updates, then $36/year extension to keep receiving updates (license stays usable but frozen if not renewed).
quote: Starter: $49... Unleashed: $109... Lifetime: $249... Annual Extension Fee for Starter and Unleashed: $36

claim: Unraid 7.3.0-rc.1 (April 22, 2026) ships Linux 6.18.23, ZFS 2.4.1, Docker v29, TPM-based licensing, internal-boot option, and exposes ZFS ARC max as a UI tunable.
quote: Linux kernel: version 6.18.23-Unraid... zfs: version 2.4.1... All new and replacement keys use TPM-based licensing when possible

claim: Unraid 7.0.0 (January 9, 2025) introduced Hybrid ZFS Pools, native LUKS encryption on ZFS, Overlay2 Docker storage on ZFS; 7.1.0 (May 2025) added foreign ZFS pool import (TrueNAS, Proxmox, QNAP); 7.2.0 (October 2025) enabled RAIDZ single-vdev expansion.
OpenMediaVault 8 'Synchrony' released December 24, 2025, rebased onto Debian 13 Trixie, AMD64/ARM64 only (32-bit dropped); WPA3 support, Time Machine quotas; OMV remains free under GPLv3.
quote: Upgrade to Debian 13 (Trixie)... WPA3 (SAE) authentication support... 32-bit support discontinued

claim: OpenMediaVault is a Debian-based NAS distribution with a web UI and plug-in API, supporting SMB/NFS/iSCSI, software RAID 0/1/4/5/6/10, S.M.A.R.T., licensed GPLv3; targeted at home and small-office NAS use.
quote: openmediavault is the next generation network attached storage (NAS) solution based on Debian Linux... Software-RAID (levels 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, and 10, plus JBOD)... primarily designed to be used in home environments or small home offices

claim: The OpenMediaVault GitHub repo has roughly 6.7k stars and 577 forks, indicating a steady but modest contributor community compared to TrueNAS or Proxmox.
quote: openmediavault is the next generation network attached storage (NAS) solution based on Debian Linux. Thanks to the modular design of the framework it can be enhanced via plugins.

claim: Proxmox VE 9.0 was released August 5, 2025, rebased on Debian 13 Trixie with kernel 6.14.8, ZFS 2.3.3 (RAIDZ expansion), QEMU 10.0.2, LXC 6.0.4, Ceph Squid 19.2.3; AGPLv3 with optional EUR 115/CPU/year enterprise repo subscription.
quote: Proxmox VE 9.0 is built upon Debian 13 'Trixie'... GNU Affero General Public License, v3... commercial subscriptions starting at EUR 115 yearly per CPU

claim: Proxmox VE 8.4 (the previous LTS-style branch) is supported until August 2026, giving roughly one year of overlap with the 9.x series for safe migration.
quote: Proxmox VE 8.4 is supported until August 2026

claim: Cockpit is the de-facto web UI for Debian/Ubuntu DIY NAS builds: single-package install, port 9090, includes cockpit-storaged for partitions/RAID/LVM/LUKS and as of v346 (Sept 2025) Stratis V2; the 45Drives modules add NFS/Samba share management.
quote: Cockpit is a web-based graphical interface for servers... cockpit-storaged handles everything from basic partition management to RAID arrays, LVM, LUKS encryption, and as of version 346 (September 2025), Stratis V2 pools

claim: NixOS supports ZFS as a first-class root and data filesystem via boot.zfs and services.zfs options, but ZFS often lags the latest mainline kernels so an LTS kernel is recommended for NAS use.
quote: ZFS often does not support the latest kernel versions, so it is recommended to use an LTS kernel version whenever possible

claim: Community sentiment in 2025 is that NixOS pays a real complexity tax — Nix language is a functional DSL, documentation is fractured between two wikis, and Flakes have been 'experimental' for years — making it suitable for declarative NAS but a poor fit for casual operators.
quote: NixOS is much more complex than traditional distributions and has a lot of historical issues. If you aren't careful, you will drown in the complexity on offer

claim: TrueNAS Scale's stock r8169 kernel driver caps Realtek RTL8125 NICs at gigabit speeds and some revisions (e.g. rev 0c) fail entirely; out-of-tree r8125 drivers must be reinstalled after every TrueNAS upgrade — a known operator pain point versus Intel NICs.
quote: the built-in kernel support only supports 1G rate, not the full 2.5G speed... every time TrueNAS version is upgraded, new drivers need to be re-installed

claim: Intel iGPU QuickSync transcoding works on TrueNAS Scale via the i915 driver and a per-app GPU allocation, but newer Intel CPUs and the LXC/Incus app stack still require manual configuration to surface /dev/dri devices for Plex/Jellyfin.
quote: The i915 kernel driver lets Linux use the built-in graphics of Intel CPUs

claim: Synology's 2025 NAS line locked storage-pool creation to Synology-branded drives in DSM 7.2; backlash and a sales drop forced a reversal in DSM 7.3 (October 2025), but health telemetry and firmware updates remain first-party-only — the controversy that drove many users toward DIY NAS OS in 2026.
quote: Synology reverses third-party HDD ban in DSM 7.3... third-party drives regain storage pool creation and core functionality

claim: iXsystems' early-2025 messaging that 'Enterprise' and 'Community' editions would diverge prompted community pushback; the company clarified the open-core model is unchanged and Community Edition remains a fully featured free product.
quote: TrueNAS Responds to Community Concerns With New Community and Enterprise Vision

claim: OpenZFS 2.3 (Jan 2025) shipped with RAIDZ Expansion, Fast Dedup, Direct I/O, longer filename support, and Linux 6.12 compatibility.
quote: OpenZFS 2.3 Released With RAIDZ Expansion, Fast Dedup, Direct I/O & Other Great Improvements

claim: RAIDZ expansion in 2.3 lets you add a single drive to an existing RAIDZ vdev; data is rewritten across all drives, the pool stays online and fault-tolerant during the (multi-day) reflow.
quote: The expansion feature lets you add another drive to an existing array, and then enlarge the array to add the extra space.

claim: OpenZFS 2.2 (Nov 2023/2024 timeframe) was the first release to actually ship RAIDZ expansion, ending the long-standing 'must destroy and recreate the vdev' constraint.
quote: OpenZFS RAID-Z Expansion: A New Era in Storage Flexibility

claim: Official OpenZFS guidance recommends 8GB+ RAM for best performance but explicitly says ZFS works fine with 2GB or less; the '1GB per TB' rule is not in the docs.
quote: 8GB+ of memory for the best performance

claim: The '1 GB RAM per TB of storage' rule is treated as folklore rather than real sizing guidance, and modern ZFS runs correctly on modest RAM (just slower).
quote: 1 GB RAM per TB of storage ... a superstition with a unit attached

claim: ECC RAM is recommended but not required for ZFS; co-creator Matt Ahrens has stated ZFS does not need ECC any more than any other filesystem, though ECC prevents in-RAM bit flips becoming silent on-disk corruption.
quote: ZFS co-creator Matt Ahrens has publicly stated that ECC is not strictly required for ZFS

claim: dRAID provides distributed parity with integrated hot spares for much faster resilver/rebuild than traditional RAIDZ; a 'special' vdev offloads metadata (and small blocks) onto fast SSDs to accelerate HDD pools.
quote: dRAID (Distributed RAID) is a vdev that provides distributed parity and hot spares across multiple drives, allowing for much faster rebuild performance after a failure.

claim: The official btrfs Status page still marks RAID5/RAID6 as 'unstable' with severe known problems and tells users not to use it for non-test workloads.
quote: do not use for other than testing purposes, known severe problems, missing implementation of some core parts

claim: Btrfs work continued through 2025; Linux 6.19 (Dec 2025) added experimental RAID5/6 fixes (block size > page size) but the write-hole-class issues are still gated behind CONFIG_BTRFS_EXPERIMENTAL.
quote: Btrfs In Linux 6.19 Adds Experimental Features, Continues Preparations For FSCRYPT

claim: Synology runs btrfs as the filesystem on top of Linux mdraid (dmraid) plus LVM2 rather than using btrfs's native RAID5/6, precisely because btrfs RAID is considered unsafe.
quote: What is the RAID implementation for Btrfs File System on Synology NAS?

claim: Bcachefs was removed from the mainline Linux kernel during the 6.17 cycle after a dispute between Kent Overstreet and Linus Torvalds over a late journal_rewind feature submission.
quote: Bcachefs removed from the mainline kernel

claim: Since kernel 6.18, bcachefs ships only as an out-of-tree DKMS module; Kent Overstreet maintains Debian/Ubuntu packages and the project is in a 'hard freeze' chasing stability before lifting the experimental label.
quote: Bcachefs goes DKMS after Torvalds' kernel banishment

claim: ext4 and XFS lack data checksums entirely, so an mdadm + ext4/xfs stack will silently return corrupted data on bit rot unless you layer dm-integrity underneath the array.
quote: Protecting Data Integrity on Ext4 and XFS with dm-integrity and LUKS

claim: SnapRAID computes parity on a schedule (not in real time), tolerates per-disk filesystems and mismatched drive sizes, and survives multi-disk loss with only the lost disks' data gone — the surviving disks are still readable as plain filesystems.
quote: Compare SnapRAID

claim: SnapRAID + mergerfs is the canonical r/DataHoarder pattern for media archives: JBOD pool with file-level redundancy, easy single-disk expansion, and survivable partial-array failures.
quote: SnapRAID - Perfect Media Server

claim: Because mergerfs stores each file whole on a single underlying disk, losing more disks than parity covers only loses the data on those specific disks; the rest of the pool remains intact, unlike ZFS where exceeding tolerance loses the entire vdev.
quote: (MergerFS + SnapRAID) is the new RAID 5

claim: btrfs send/receive replicates read-only snapshots between filesystems incrementally, transferring only the diff between a parent snapshot and the new one.
quote: btrfs-send(8) — BTRFS documentation

claim: Synology SHR disks can be mounted on vanilla Linux but require mdadm assembly + LVM activation, and modern kernels (5.4+) break Synology's old btrfs format, often forcing use of an Ubuntu Bionic 4.15 kernel.
quote: modern Linux kernel (5.4+) doesn't support the old btrfs version used by Synology... sudo mdadm --assemble --run /dev/md0 /dev/sdb5 ... sudo mount /dev/mapper/vg1000-lv /mnt/synology

claim: Synology DSM layers mdadm software RAID under LVM under btrfs/ext4, so reading SHR disks elsewhere requires installing both mdadm and lvm2 to traverse all three layers.
quote: Synology SHR under the hood uses LVM (Logical Volume Manager) to manage the disks ... a combination of partitions, software raid (using mdadm) and logical volumes (using lvm)

claim: ZFS pools created in TrueNAS are based on OpenZFS and import cleanly into vanilla Debian/Ubuntu provided enabled feature flags are supported on the destination kernel module version.
quote: ZFS pools are compatible between FreeBSD and Linux because OpenZFS uses the same on-disk format

claim: OpenZFS feature flags gate cross-platform import: a pool with newly-enabled flags will refuse to import on a system whose ZFS module does not implement them, which is the principal ZFS lock-in vector.
quote: Feature flags are used to tag features with unique names to provide portability between OpenZFS implementations running on different platforms, as long as all of the feature flags enabled on the ZFS pool are supported by both platforms

claim: TrueNAS Scale 24.10 Electric Eel ripped out the K3s/Helm app stack and replaced it with native Docker + Compose; legacy Helm-chart catalogs (TrueCharts) are deprecated and require running K3s inside a Sandbox.
quote: the Apps infrastructure from K3S to Docker. Docker and Compose will simplify portability and innovation of Apps

claim: Apps installed on TrueNAS 23.10 (Cobia) and 24.04 (Dragonfish) had a hard June 1, 2025 cutoff after which automatic migration to the Docker-based Electric Eel app system stopped working, leaving users to redeploy by hand.
quote: On June 1st, 2025, an update to the TrueNAS Apps Catalog will enable per-App IP addressing and this update will also break the ability to automatically migrate Apps from Cobia (23.10) and Dragonfish (24.04) to Electric Eel

claim: Unraid array members are individually formatted (XFS default, ext4/btrfs/ZFS optional) with no striping, so any single data disk can be removed and read on a plain Linux box.
quote: Unraid supports six filesystems for array and cache storage... Linux systems can read these file systems natively

claim: The NonRAID project ports Unraid's md_unraid kernel driver to vanilla Debian/Ubuntu, demonstrating the array driver itself is portable but warning that partition-scheme differences and superblock metadata make existing Unraid arrays only experimentally importable.
quote: a fork of the unRAID system's open-source md_unraid kernel driver... differences in how UnRAID might have partitioned the disks...can lead to size mismatches that prevent proper import

claim: Unraid stores Docker container configuration as VM XML templates on the boot device with appdata on a dedicated share, so containers themselves are portable, while plugins (a separate mechanism) are Unraid-specific and not portable.
quote: Features that cannot be provided as Docker containers should use plugins. It's advisable to avoid using plugins for general-purpose applications that can run safely in isolated containers

claim: OpenMediaVault keeps its entire system state in /etc/openmediavault/config.xml and renders service configs via the omv-salt tool driving Salt states under /srv/salt/omv/, meaning underlying Samba/NFS configs are still standard Debian files but get overwritten on every commit.
quote: omv-salt is used to deploy the configuration of services... openmediavault SLS files are located at /srv/salt/omv/

claim: OMV has no built-in config export/restore tool: maintainers say there is no reliable way to apply a saved config.xml to a fresh install, so portability across hardware is by full disk image rather than config replay.
quote: There is no regular backup/restore procedure, but you can keep the file /etc/openmediavault/config.xml for reference

claim: OMV is officially installable as a package set on top of an existing Debian server (keyring + apt repo), confirming it is genuinely a thin layer rather than a custom distribution, but it does overwrite any pre-existing service configs it manages.
quote: openmediavault does not import any existing settings of your system except for network and time related settings, and existing settings will be overwritten if the service is managed by openmediavault

claim: Proxmox LXC containers are standard LXC under the hood and migrate via vzdump/restore, but live migration is unsupported (restart-migrate only) and storage type assumptions in storage.cfg can complicate moves between nodes with different backends.
quote: Running containers cannot live-migrated due to technical limitations. You can do a restart migration

claim: Proxmox VE in late 2025 added native OCI image support so Docker images can be pulled and run directly inside LXC, narrowing the historical Docker-in-VM workaround and improving container-format portability.
quote: you can directly pull down OCI compliant container images from the regular registries that we use for running docker container images

claim: Proxmox VMs are stored as standard qcow2/raw disks and vzdump-produced VMA archives can be restored on plain KVM/QEMU outside Proxmox, so the VM layer itself is highly portable; the lock-in is the storage.cfg/cluster definitions, not the disks.
quote: Proxmox VE backups are always full backups, containing the VM/CT configuration and all data

claim: Samba on TrueNAS keeps its user database in passdb.tdb, and migrating SMB users between systems requires manually re-adding them or copying the tdb (with caveats); LDAP Samba schema support was removed in 24.10, forcing migration to AD for centralized identity.
quote: Support for LDAP Samba Schema is deprecated in TrueNAS 22.02 and removed in 24.10

claim: Real-world reports confirm a TrueNAS-built zpool can be exported, imported into Ubuntu OpenZFS, and round-tripped back, but ZoL writes Linux-style xattrs that may strip SMB metadata when crossing FreeBSD/Linux and NFSv4 ACLs do not survive the trip.
quote: ZoL writes xattrs with incompatible names (causing files written over SMB to lose metadata on pool import), and ZoL in SCALE does not currently support NFSv4 ACLs

claim: Synology SHR with btrfs is read on vanilla Linux by activating the LVM volume group (vgchange -ay vg1) and mounting the resulting btrfs LV; the underlying stack is mdadm/dmraid + LVM2 + btrfs, not native btrfs RAID.
quote: Synology uses this to their advantage, using a number of standard Linux tools, including Device Mapper RAID rather than btrfs RAID... I could simply plug all the disks into my desktop and mount the array.

claim: A documented Synology→Unraid migration used mdadm -AsfR plus 'vgchange -ay --activationmode partial' to mount a single SHR disk read-only inside an Ubuntu VM, then transferred via 'rsync -a --info=progress2' at ~140 MB/s before adding the second disk as parity.
quote: rsync -a --info=progress2 ./synology/ ./diskstation/ ... approximately 140MB/sec throughput across the virtual driver stack.

claim: Hyper Backup writes to a proprietary deduplicated HBK container that is only accessible via Synology tooling (Hyper Backup, HB Vault, or DSM bare-metal restore) — no open reader exists.
quote: the backup on the destination side will be in Hyper Backup's proprietary format (HBK), and accessing it and restoring from it will be possible using the Hyper Backup, HB Vault, or bare metal restore.

claim: TrueNAS Scale's official third-party migration path mounts the source NAS read-only over SMB and uses Syncthing Enterprise (24.04+) to preserve metadata; SMB alternate data streams arrived in 24.10. Share/user definitions are not imported — local accounts referenced in ACLs must be re-created on TrueNAS first.
quote: All accounts referenced in NFSv4 ACLs and Windows Security Descriptors must be available on the TrueNAS server... Plan for one-way migration of data from the source to the TrueNAS target. Remote shares must be mounted read-only.

claim: ZFS send/receive streams are portable across OpenZFS platforms but feature-gated; encrypted datasets must be sent with -w (raw), and once a non-raw receive lands, no further raw incrementals can be received into that dataset.
quote: ZFS will not allow a mix of raw receives and non-raw receives. Specifically, any raw incremental receives that are attempted after a non-raw receive will fail.

claim: btrfs send/receive requires read-only snapshots and exact UUID/state matching for incrementals; protocol v2 (compressed-data passthrough) requires btrfs-progs >=6.0 and kernel >=6.0 on the sender, and receive does not validate untrusted streams.
quote: Receive does not currently do a very good job of validating that an incremental send stream actually makes sense... users are advised to not use btrfs receive on send streams from untrusted sources.

claim: For NAS migration rsync needs -aAXH (archive + ACLs + xattrs + hardlinks); rsync <3.1.2 may drop NFSv4 ACLs, and Synology's bundled rsync historically ships without ACL/xattr support so the pull must originate from a modern Linux box.
quote: The -A (or --acls) option causes rsync to update the destination ACLs to be the same as the source ACLs, and also implies --perms. The -X (or --xattrs) option causes rsync to update the destination extended attributes to be the same as the source ones.

claim: Community consensus for Synology→TrueNAS bulk transfer is rsync over SSH inside tmux, or mounting the Synology share over SMB from TrueNAS; Hyper Backup's rsync 'Single Copy' mode works but has been observed sitting at 100% for ~a week before finalizing on multi-TB sets.
quote: Synology Hyper Backup with the RSync Single Copy option takes a relatively long time and after about a week at 100%, it does finish.

claim: Active Backup for Business backup repositories require a Synology NAS running ABB to restore — the format is not portable; granular file/folder restore can export individual files but the backup catalog itself is DSM-locked.
quote: Active Backup for Business

claim: A community Synology→TrueNAS migration toolkit exists that automates SMB-based pulls and documents share/permission re-creation steps, illustrating that share definitions and SMB user databases must be rebuilt by hand on the new stack.
quote: Synology to TrueNAS

claim: Proxmox users in 2025 still hit btrfs-to-btrfs migration failures ('cannot flip ro->rw with received_uuid set'), confirming that btrfs send/receive remains brittle for moving subvolumes between hosts versus the more mature ZFS send/recv path.
quote: cannot migrate from storage type 'btrfs' to 'btrfs'

claim: TrueNAS Scale 24.10 Electric Eel replaced the Kubernetes (k3s) Apps backend with Docker/Docker Compose, with breaking changes that made June 1, 2025 the deadline for automatic apps migration on upgrade.
quote: The TrueNAS Apps feature backend moves from Kubernetes to Docker to streamline App deployment and management... June 1, 2025 is the deadline for automatic apps migration on upgrade

claim: Users upgrading TrueNAS Scale to Cobia and Dragonfish reported apps stuck deploying or completely gone, often requiring deletion of the ix-applications dataset and full reset.
quote: All apps gone after upgrade to Cobia

claim: The Cobia-to-Dragonfish update broke k3s for many users, with apps left in a non-working state until manual intervention.
quote: K3s broke after update to Dragonfish-24.04.1.1

claim: TrueNAS provides scheduled snapshot and replication tasks via the GUI, automatically creating a periodic snapshot task before each replication run if one does not already exist, and supporting SSH-based push/pull to remote pools.
quote: TrueNAS typically creates a periodic snapshot task right before it performs the replication task if one is not already created for the task.

claim: TrueNAS ships with built-in S.M.A.R.T. and pool alerts whose default severity is Immediately, sending email (or other configured channels) as soon as a SMART error is detected once email is configured.
quote: Alert frequency options are Immediately (Default), Hourly, Daily or Never.

claim: ServeTheHome reported a strong community backlash against the Unraid pricing model, with users threatening to migrate to TrueNAS and disputing Unraid's claim that it is not a subscription.
quote: People will flock to truenas... Ahh the subscription model. The best thing that has brainwashed people since sliced bread. ... Nope, do not support subscriptions and never will. Truenas it is.

claim: Unraid's parity-protected array does not natively snapshot, so snapshots are only available on ZFS pools or on individual ZFS-formatted array drives, which lack the multi-disk pool's redundancy and self-healing.
quote: ZFS disks in the array are managed individually; you do not get the combined performance, redundancy, or self-healing of a true multi-disk ZFS pool.

claim: Snapshot/replication on Unraid in practice relies on community user scripts (e.g. SpaceinvaderOne's Unraid_ZFS_Dataset_Snapshot_and_Replications) rather than a first-party scheduled task UI.
quote: Unraid_ZFS_Dataset_Snapshot_and_Replications ... a comprehensive solution for snapshotting and replicating ZFS datasets, either locally, to a remote server on the same network, or over the internet.

claim: Reviewers find Unraid's Community Apps store dramatically more beginner-friendly than TrueNAS Scale's Docker workflow, with thousands of pre-configured containers vs. TrueNAS containers being 'way more complicated'.
quote: Docker containers were way more complicated than I was used to, and setting up shares sometimes worked but not always. ... Unraid has thousands of containers ready to go out of the box. Most of the containers are pre-configured for you

claim: Unraid Community Applications acts as the de facto app store for Docker containers and plugins, with curation by moderators and over 2000 entries, installable directly from the WebGUI.
quote: the easiest and most popular way to discover, install, and manage both Docker containers and plugins on your Unraid server, providing an app store-like experience

claim: Running TrueNAS as a VM on top of Proxmox-managed ZFS is widely warned against because nested ZFS-on-ZFS overhead compounds; standard advice is HBA passthrough to the NAS guest or running Samba directly on the Proxmox host (with no NAS GUI).
quote: ZFS has terrible overhead that multiplies exponentially, making it not a good idea to run ZFS on top of ZFS, which is what you need for TrueNAS.

claim: Running Docker inside an LXC on Proxmox requires enabling nesting and keyctl, which exposes /sys and /proc to the container; Proxmox upstream still officially recommends running Docker in a VM rather than nested in LXC.
quote: Enabling nesting is not recommended as it will expose /sys and /proc of your host with read-write permissions inside the container, and this setup could lead to container breakout in some cases.

claim: Proxmox VE 9.1 added first-class support for OCI/Docker images as application containers via LXC, reducing the need for nesting hacks for self-hosted Docker workloads on the hypervisor host.
quote: Proxmox VE 9.1 provides support for OCI images as application containers, allowing you to run Docker containers through Proxmox VE directly, which get "translated" to LXC containers.

claim: On plain Debian+ZFS, zrepl is the de facto end-to-end snapshot+replication daemon; it runs as a continuous service with its own scheduling, automatic resumable send/receive, and pruning rules — replacing the cron-driven zfs-auto-snapshot model.
quote: zrepl is a one-stop ZFS backup & replication solution... runs as a continuous service and does its own scheduling, rather than using cron.

claim: ZFS-on-Debian's most reported post-upgrade pain is unattended kernel updates leaving the new kernel's initrd without ZFS modules, which prevents the boot pool from importing and drops the system to an initramfs shell.
quote: Debian/Root on ZFS: modules missing from initrd after kernel update

claim: Recovering ZFS-on-Debian after a bad kernel/dkms upgrade typically requires booting a live environment, importing pools read-only first, then rebuilding zfs-dkms against the new kernel - a workflow notably absent from appliance distros like TrueNAS.
quote: boot a live environment with ZFS tooling, import pools read-only first to check if they import successfully

claim: OpenMediaVault's container story is the openmediavault-compose plugin from omv-extras (not first-party), which manages docker-compose stacks via the OMV WebGUI on top of Debian's Docker.
quote: (Docker) Compose Plugin For OMV7

claim: OMV 7→8 upgrades reportedly break the docker-compose plugin: shared-folder paths get unmapped, containers disappear from the UI, and a fix7to8upgrade script is required to recover state.
quote: Docker containers don't show up after OMV upgrade 7 -> 8

claim: OMV's notification system is built on Postfix and integrates SMART, MDADM, scheduled-task and cron-apt events, but email must be manually configured under System | Notification before any alert is sent.
quote: OpenMediaVault's notification system is integrated into several services including scheduled tasks, services monitoring, S.M.A.R.T., MDADM and cron-apt, using Postfix as the mail backend.

claim: Scrutiny is the cross-stack drive-monitoring fallback (deployable as a Docker container on TrueNAS, Unraid, OMV, Proxmox or plain Debian) that overlays smartd with a web UI, historical trend charts, real-world failure thresholds, and webhook/email/Slack/Telegram alerts.
quote: Hard Drive S.M.A.R.T Monitoring, Historical Trends & Real World Failure Thresholds
